 1998년 10월 27일, 미국 상원은 [디지털 밀레니엄 저작권법](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act)을 통과시켰습니다. 이 법은 저작권 소유자의 권리를 보호하기 위해 디자인되었습니다. 이 법의 일부로, 저작권 소유자는 온라인 서비스 제공업체(OSP)에게 저작권이 있는 자료가 사이트에서 사용되고 있다고 생각되면 이를 제거하도록 요청할 수 있습니다.

 저작권 소유자가 GitHub에 타당하고 좋은 신고를 제출하면, 법적으로 [안전항구 조항](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/17/512)에 따라 보호받을 수 있습니다. 안전한 항구 조항은 OSP가 사용자가 게시한 자료의 저작권 침해를 모니터링하지 않는 한, OSP가 저작권 침해에 대한 책임을 지지 않도록 합니다.

 GitHub의 [DMCA 정책](https://docs.github.com/ko/site-policy/dmca-policy)은 GitHub가 미국 저작권법 및 국제 지적 재산 협약 및 조약을 준수하는 방법을 설명합니다. GitHub의 정책은 DMCA 통지서를 제출하거나 GitHub에 게시된 자료에 대한 카운터 통지를 제출하려는 경우 따라야 할 절차를 설명합니다.

### GitHub의 DMCA 통지서 처리

GitHub은 저작권 소유자의 권리를 존중합니다. 동시에, GitHub은 사용자가 잘못된 DMCA 통지서로 인해 프로젝트가 중단되는 것을 원하지 않습니다. DMCA 통지서가 제출되면 GitHub은 다음과 같은 단계를 수행합니다.

1. GitHub은 통지서를 검토하여 유효성을 확인합니다. 유효한 통지서에는 [DMCA 정책](https://docs.github.com/ko/site-policy/dmca-policy)에 설명된 대로 특정 정보가 포함되어야 합니다.
2. 통지서가 유효한 경우, GitHub은 해당 자료를 비활성화하고, 해당 자료를 게시한 사용자에게 통지서 사본을 제공합니다.
3. 해당 자료를 게시한 사용자는 GitHub에 카운터 통지를 제출하여 해당 자료가 잘못 제거되었다고 주장할 수 있습니다. GitHub은 카운터 통지를 검토하여 유효성을 확인합니다. 유효한 카운터 통지에는 [DMCA 정책](https://docs.github.com/ko/site-policy/dmca-policy)에 설명된 대로 특정 정보가 포함되어야 합니다.
4. 카운터 통지가 유효한 경우, GitHub은 해당 자료를 복원하고, 원본 통지서를 제출한 사람에게 카운터 통지서 사본을 제공합니다. 원본 통지서를 제출한 사람은 10영업일 이내에 GitHub을 상대로 소송을 제기하여 해당 자료가 저작권을 침해한다고 주장해야 합니다. 그렇지 않으면 GitHub은 해당 자료를 영구적으로 복원합니다.

Next, install the [Debugger for Chrome](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=msjsdiag.debugger-for-chrome) extension from the [Visual Studio Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/vscode).

## 3. Set up your launch configuration

The `launch.json` file you created in step 1 already contains a basic launch configuration. However, you may need to customize it based on your project's needs. Here are some common customizations:

- **URL**: Change the `url` property to match the URL of your application.
- **Port**: If your application runs on a different port, update the `url` property accordingly.
- **Source maps**: If you're using source maps, you may need to configure the `sourceMaps` property.
- **Path mapping**: If your source files are located in a different directory than the compiled files, you may need to configure the `pathMapping` property.

## 4. Start debugging

To start debugging your application:

1. Open the file you want to debug in the editor.
2. Set a breakpoint by clicking in the gutter next to the line number.
3. Press `F5` or click the "Start Debugging" button in the Debug view.

The Debugger for Chrome extension will launch a new instance of Chrome and attach the debugger to it. You can now step through your code, inspect variables, and use all the other debugging features provided by Visual Studio Code.

## 5. Advanced debugging techniques

Here are some advanced debugging techniques you can use with the Debugger for Chrome extension:

- **Conditional breakpoints**: Right-click on a breakpoint and select "Edit Breakpoint" to add a condition.
- **Logpoints**: Right-click on a breakpoint and select "Edit Breakpoint" to add a log message.
- **Call stack**: Use the Call Stack view to see the sequence of function calls that led to the current execution point.
- **Watch expressions**: Add expressions to the Watch view to monitor their values as you step through your code.

## 6. Troubleshooting

If you encounter issues while debugging, here are some common troubleshooting steps:

- **Chrome not launching**: Make sure Chrome is installed on your system and that the Debugger for Chrome extension is up to date.
- **Breakpoints not hitting**: Check that your source maps are correctly configured and that the `webRoot` property in your launch configuration is set to the correct directory.
- **Debugger not attaching**: Try closing all instances of Chrome and restarting the debugging session.

## Conclusion

## 1. Introduction

The field of artificial intelligence (AI) has seen tremendous growth in recent years, with applications ranging from natural language processing to computer vision. One of the key challenges in AI is the development of models that can understand and generate human-like text. This has led to the creation of large language models (LLMs), which are trained on vast amounts of text data to learn the patterns and structures of human language.

LLMs have shown remarkable performance in a variety of tasks, including text generation, translation, summarization, and question answering. However, their size and complexity make them computationally expensive to train and deploy. This has led to the development of smaller, more efficient models that can be fine-tuned for specific tasks.

In this paper, we present a study on the performance of a fine-tuned LLM on a specific task. We evaluate the model's ability to generate coherent and relevant text, and compare its performance to that of a baseline model. Our results show that the fine-tuned model outperforms the baseline model in terms of both coherence and relevance.

## 2. Related Work

There has been a significant amount of research on the development and evaluation of LLMs. Some of the most notable models include GPT-3 [1], BERT [2], and T5 [3]. These models have achieved state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of NLP tasks.

However, the size and complexity of these models make them difficult to deploy in resource-constrained environments. This has led to the development of smaller, more efficient models that can be fine-tuned for specific tasks. Some examples of these models include DistilBERT [4] and TinyBERT [5].

## 3. Methodology

In this study, we fine-tune a pre-trained LLM on a specific task. We use a dataset of text samples that are relevant to the task, and fine-tune the model using a standard fine-tuning procedure. We then evaluate the model's performance on a held-out test set.

### 3.1. Dataset

We use a dataset of text samples that are relevant to the task. The dataset is split into training, validation, and test sets. The training set is used to fine-tune the model, the validation set is used to monitor the model's performance during training, and the test set is used to evaluate the model's final performance.

### 3.2. Model Architecture

We use a pre-trained LLM as the base model for our fine-tuning. The model is a transformer-based model with multiple layers of self-attention and feed-forward neural networks. The model is pre-trained on a large corpus of text data using a self-supervised learning objective.

### 3.3. Fine-Tuning Procedure

We fine-tune the model using a standard fine-tuning procedure. We initialize the model with the pre-trained weights, and then train the model on the training set using a supervised learning objective. We monitor the model's performance on the validation set during training, and stop training when the model's performance on the validation set stops improving.

### 3.4. Evaluation Metrics

We evaluate the model's performance using a variety of metrics, including perplexity, BLEU score, and human evaluation. Perplexity is a measure of how well the model predicts the next word in a sequence of text. BLEU score is a measure of how similar the model's generated text is to a reference text. Human evaluation involves having human evaluators rate the quality of the model's generated text.

## 4. Results

We present the results of our experiments in this section. We compare the performance of the fine-tuned model to that of a baseline model, which is the pre-trained LLM without any fine-tuning.

### 4.1. Perplexity

We evaluate the model's performance using perplexity on the test set. The results are shown in Table 1.

|--------|-------------|

Table 1: Perplexity on the test set.

As shown in Table 1, the fine-tuned model achieves a lower perplexity than the baseline model, indicating that it is better at predicting the next word in a sequence of text.

### 4.2. BLEU Score

We evaluate the model's performance using BLEU score on the test set. The results are shown in Table 2.

| Model | BLEU Score |
|--------|-------------|
| Baseline | 20.5 |
| Fine-tuned | 25.3 |

Table 2: BLEU score on the test set.

As shown in Table 2, the fine-tuned model achieves a higher BLEU score than the baseline model, indicating that it generates text that is more similar to the reference text.
